memories.memory_text: I told Ted to stop staring at Christy—some things are out of our league, like filet mignon for hamburger guys. I could see him itching to say something dumb, so I hit him with the truth, juicy and raw.
grounded_facts: Charlie approaches Ted in the school corridor while Christy is across the hall.
Charlie uses a food analogy comparing desirable girls to filet mignon and average guys like himself and Ted to ground chuck.
Charlie references his background as a butcher's son through the beef analogy.
Ted does not respond verbally to Charlie's analogy in the scene.

memories.scene_title: 7、INT. BASKETBALL COURT - TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - DAY
memories.memory_text: I flipped open 'HEAVEN' and did my Ginger voice—'set it on VIBRATE, honey'—winking at Ted like I was handing him the keys to the kingdom. His face? Priceless.
grounded_facts: Charlie shows Ted the nudie magazine 'HEAVEN' with a woman named Ginger on the centerfold.
Charlie imitates Ginger in a feminine voice, delivering a sexually suggestive line about vibration and lube.
Charlie winks at Ted while saying 'Don't knock it till you tried it.'

memories.scene_title: 7、INT. BASKETBALL COURT - TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - DAY
memories.memory_text: I saw that look in Ted’s eyes—the dumb, dreamy kind—and I started yelling 'Danger, Will Robinson, danger' to stop him. He didn’t listen. He just walked away.
grounded_facts: Charlie observes a determined and dreamy look in Ted's eyes before he approaches Christy.
Charlie warns Ted by saying 'Danger, Will Robinson, danger' as a clear attempt to stop him.
Ted ignores the warning and walks away defiantly.

memories.scene_title: 10、INT. TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - MEN'S ROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I hit Ted with the one-word joke—'MEATLOAF'—right after he puked in the sink, and the next thing I know he's staring at himself in the mirror like the word just cracked open the universe.
grounded_facts: Charlie says 'One word, my friend. One word.' followed by 'MEATLOAF.'
Ted is at the sink, having just vomited, and looks into the mirror after Charlie speaks.
Ted responds with existential distress, saying, 'There's gotta be more to life than meatloaf.'

memories.memory_text: I was just standing there, probably looking at one of those dorky family photos, when Ted’s mom suddenly throws my name into the ring like I’m some kind of babysitter for his will-reading trip.
grounded_facts: Charlie was present in the Nelson living room during the conversation about the will reading.
Ted's mother suggested Charlie as a travel companion for Ted to the will reading.
The suggestion was made while she was pouring coffee, and Charlie was not the one who proposed the idea.

memories.memory_text: I remember leaning in close, my voice low but sharp—'Who the hell was your father?'—because one second we were joking about names, and the next this stunning driver was hugging Ted like he was grieving some dead legend.
grounded_facts: Charlie and Ted entered the LAX terminal together.
A chauffeur holding a 'TED NELSON' sign greeted Ted.
The chauffeur expressed condolences for Ted's father's death.
Ted appeared stunned by the greeting.
Charlie whispered a question to Ted: 'Who the hell was your father?'

memories.memory_text: I cranked up the rock music just to see Ted freak out—he told me not to touch anything, but come on, what’s a limo without a soundtrack?
grounded_facts: Charlie activates the music system on the control console despite Ted's warning.
The music is loud and sudden, changing the atmosphere in the limo.
Charlie speaks loudly over the music, shouting his line about 'the life' and 'spring break'.

memories.memory_text: I popped the Dom Perignon and got drenched in champagne—foam everywhere, right down my pants—and yeah, it was messy, but damn, did it feel like we’d stolen the good life for a minute.
grounded_facts: Charlie opens the bar and retrieves a bottle of Dom Perignon.
He ignores Ted's objection about cost and insists it's part of the experience.
The champagne overflows and spills on Charlie's crotch after the cork pops.
He uses a bar towel to clean up while still grinning.

memories.memory_text: I stuck my head out of the limo sunroof, wind blasting my face, and yelled, 'This is how the other half lives, bro—enjoy it while you can,' while Ted looked like he might puke or pass out.
grounded_facts: Charlie sticks his head out of the limo sunroof after Ted.
Sun is on their faces and wind is in their hair.
Charlie speaks the line: 'This is how the other half lives, bro. Enjoy it while you can.'

memories.memory_text: I gawked so hard my jaw nearly hit the table when Zukerman said Ted owned the whole 'Heaven' empire—like, every magazine, theme park, and glittery piece of real estate. I couldn’t help but blurt out, 'Everything?' before anyone could shut me down.
grounded_facts: Charlie gawks in reaction to Ted's inheritance.
Charlie asks about the scope of the inheritance: publishing, real estate, retail, theme parks.
Zukerman confirms the inheritance is vast ('as far as the eye can see').

memories.memory_text: When the screen lit up with Ted’s bio parents—especially his mom, practically spilling out of that gold top—I couldn’t resist cracking a joke under my breath about where Ted got his taste in girls, just loud enough for Billy to hear.
grounded_facts: A monitor displays Ted's biological parents, Heather and Tom Deeds.
Heather is described as stunningly sexy, wearing a gold sequined halter top.
Charlie makes a crude, humorous comment about Ted's attraction to girls, implied to be for Billy's amusement.

memories.memory_text: This place is my wet dream come true—women everywhere, topless, touching, and I don’t even care that Ted’s here ruining the vibe.
grounded_facts: Charlie says
This is like my every wet dream come true,
while observing women at the pool.
He adds
I don't even care that you're here, too, bro,
directly to Ted.

memories.memory_text: I couldn’t help shouting it—‘Oh baby, yes, yes, yes. Fuck me like you mean it’—while watching her rub lotion on her tits, and Ted had to elbow me back to reality.
grounded_facts: Charlie exclaims
Oh baby, yes, yes, yes. Fuck me like you mean it,
while staring at a woman applying lotion to her breasts.
Ted physically elbows Charlie in response to his outburst.

memories.memory_text: I kicked open Ted's door waving the binoculars, yelling for him to come see something—couldn't wait, didn't care who he was talking to.
grounded_facts: Charlie enters Ted's bedroom suddenly while Ted is on the phone.
Charlie carries binoculars and says 'Check this out. Hurry!'
Charlie interrupts Ted's emotional conversation with his parents.

memories.memory_text: I flipped the lights off and shoved the binoculars into Ted’s hands, hissing for him to check it out—like I was handing him holy scripture instead of glass and plastic.
grounded_facts: Charlie turns off the light before speaking. Charlie says
Hurry" and "Check it out!

to Ted. Charlie physically hands binoculars to Ted and directs him toward the window. The action happens at night in Ted's bedroom overlooking the pool. Charlie speaks with urgency and dramatic emphasis.

memories.memory_text: When he said we were dead and this was heaven, I didn’t laugh—instead, I ran with it, picturing a room made entirely of chocolate like it was the most obvious damn thing in the afterlife.
grounded_facts: Ted suggests they are dead and in heaven after seeing Ginger. Charlie responds by imagining a room made completely of chocolate. The response builds on Ted’s fantasy rather than dismissing it. Charlie does not contradict or mock the idea of being in heaven. The exchange occurs immediately after Ted lowers the binoculars.

memories.memory_text: I yanked open the drawer and there they were—red silk panties, soft as sin. I held them up, inhaled that powdery scent, and said, 'Cha-ching. Jackpot.'
grounded_facts: Charlie opens a dresser drawer filled with Ted's mother's lingerie.
Charlie finds a pair of red silk panties and physically interacts with them by caressing and inhaling their fragrance.
Charlie says 'Cha-ching. Jackpot' upon discovery.

memories.memory_text: Ted snatched them from me, ripped them in half, and gave me that look—like I'd crossed a line. When the butler walked in, I just shrugged and said, 'A couple of boys fighting over a pair of red silk panties is nothing compared to what he's seen.'
grounded_facts: Ted grabs the panties from Charlie, causing them to rip in half.
The English butler enters the room and observes the situation.
Ted and Charlie hide the torn panty halves behind their backs.
After the butler leaves, Charlie makes a dismissive comment about the situation involving the butler's likely experiences.

memories.memory_text: I stood there watching Ted strut down the stairs with those twins on his arms, head high like he owned the place — and damn, he almost did. I couldn’t stop grinning; it was like watching a nerdy kid turn into a god right before my eyes.
grounded_facts: Charlie watched Ted descend the staircase with pride.
Ted was flanked by Kiki and Kelly, identical twins, during his entrance.
Charlie was observing from a distance and reacted emotionally to Ted's transformation.

memories.memory_text: I spun through the crowd, disco-ready, and shouted in his ear — 'A week ago you couldn’t get to second base, now you OWN second base!' — then we slapped hands like we’d won the damn Super Bowl.
grounded_facts: Charlie danced up to Ted on the dance floor.
Charlie shouted a specific phrase referencing Ted's sexual progress.
Charlie and Ted performed a high-five after the exchange.

memories.memory_text: I hopped out of that heavenly blue chopper right after Ted, planting a big kiss on the blonde pilot just so everyone could see—felt like we’d finally arrived, like we were untouchable.
grounded_facts: Charlie exits the helicopter after Ted
Charlie kisses the blonde pilot in full view of onlookers
The helicopter has a heavenly blue paint job with white clouds
Students are watching the arrival
Charlie is dressed in a flashy
Mafioso-inspired outfit

memories.memory_text: Julie called me 'Don Corleone' and I shot back about Italian loafers—then offered to show her a hickey from Miss December, which totally grossed her out.
grounded_facts: Julie addresses Charlie as 'Don Corleone'
Charlie responds with a smug remark about thousand-dollar loafers
Charlie mentions a hickey from Miss December
Julie groans in response
The exchange occurs during their walk toward the school entrance

memories.memory_text: I called myself a 'very cunning linguist' and the next thing I knew, the flight attendant was yanking me toward the washroom like I’d just won a backstage pass.
grounded_facts: Charlie introduces himself as a photographer for 'Heaven' magazine and a 'very cunning linguist'.
The flight attendant pulls Charlie toward the washroom immediately after his line.

memories.memory_text: I was handing my 'Heaven' flyer to Judy when her dad came storming in, knocking the whole stack out of my hands—next thing I know, they're floating down like some divine intervention, landing right at the feet of a group of hot girls who couldn't wait to grab them.
grounded_facts: Charlie was holding a stack of flyers and interacting with Judy.
Principal Merkin interrupted, grabbed Judy, and knocked the flyers out of Charlie's hands.
The flyers floated over the railing and landed near a group of young women who picked them up.

memories.memory_text: I saw Christy Malone turn around and say 'Hi, Teddy Bear' like it was nothing, and my whole body just locked up—cold, like I'd been slapped with a slab of frozen meat.
grounded_facts: Christy Malone appears in Ted's office and greets Ted.
Charlie is present and reacts with visible shock.
Charlie uses a metaphor involving refrigerated sirloin tip to describe the shock.

memories.memory_text: I called out 'Don't forget your broom!' after Ginger as she stormed off—couldn't help it, the witch gag was just too easy.
grounded_facts: Charlie said 'Don't forget your broom!' to Ginger as she left angrily.
Ginger was upset and asked Charlie to take her to the airport.

memories.memory_text: I leaned in and told Ted, 'Take your pick. They're IDENTICAL,' right after one of the Taylor Triplets gave him a look—he couldn't resist that.
grounded_facts: Charlie whispered to Ted to take his pick of the Taylor Triplets.
One of the Taylor Triplets batted her lashes at Ted before Charlie spoke.

memories.memory_text: I remember yelling that I loved those springy head puppies while Ted was on the phone ordering a mountain of junk, champagne in hand, the TV flashing gaudy gnomes like we were living in a fever dream.
grounded_facts: Charlie expresses enthusiasm for the springy head puppies during Ted's shopping spree on the Home Shopping Channel.
The scene takes place in Ted's luxurious hotel suite during a wild, out-of-control party.
Ted is drinking Dom Perignon and ordering items over the phone while Charlie and others watch.

memories.memory_text: I was already cranking the motor drive on my Nikon when Ted said 'Get them together,' and something just lit up in my chest—like, this was the moment, the real show was starting.
grounded_facts: Charlie is using a Nikon camera with a motor drive during the photo shoot.
Ted instructs Charlie to 'Get them together,' prompting Charlie's visible excitement.
Charlie responds with enthusiasm, saying 'Brilliant!' and directing Molly to give more for Principal Merkin.

memories.memory_text: I held up the magazine and said, 'Holy stinkers, what were you thinking?'—then laughed because the whole hallway was already on fire and I wasn't about to let Ted's sad eyes stop me from throwing one more match.
grounded_facts: Charlie speaks directly to Ted after seeing the new issue of 'Heaven'.
Charlie says 'Holy stinkers, what were you thinking?' in front of peers.
Charlie is present with a copy of the magazine in hand.
The jocks are already laughing at the magazine's content before Charlie speaks.

memories.memory_text: I handed Julie the copy of 'Heaven' with a grin, telling her 'Page three' like it was the punchline to a joke only we were in on.
grounded_facts: Charlie hands Julie a copy of the magazine 'Heaven'.
Charlie says 'Page three' to Julie upon handing it over.
Charlie is grinning during the exchange.

memories.memory_text: I saw her eyes get all watery reading the dedication, then the next second she's flipping to the pictorial and cracking up at Merkin in a wig—singing Barbra Streisand no less, with Molly practically draped over him.
grounded_facts: Julie reads a dedication in the magazine that brings tears to her eyes.
Julie laughs after seeing photos of Principal Merkin in drag at a nightclub.
The magazine includes incriminating photos of Merkin in a series of shots with Molly in the foreground.
Charlie observes Julie’s emotional reaction and the content she reacts to.

memories.memory_text: I slapped on my best grin and hit Ted with that 'coxswain on the ship of life' line—classic Charlie—watched his face go from dead to slightly less dead.
grounded_facts: Charlie delivered a humorous, life-affirming speech to Ted using the metaphor of being a coxswain.
The speech was intended to cheer Ted up during a moment of sadness at the party.
Charlie referenced his own influence by saying, 'I taught him that' after Jimmy's crude toast.

memories.memory_text: When Ted and Julie started dancing to 'People,' I grabbed Jimmy and fake-sobbed, 'I'm a sucker for a happy ending,' then yelled 'Hold me' like it was the final act of a soap opera.
grounded_facts: Charlie reacted emotionally and theatrically to Ted and Julie's reunion by hugging Jimmy.
He explicitly said, 'I'm a sucker for a happy ending,' followed by 'Hold me,' in a performative, exaggerated tone.
The moment was public, playful, and involved physical contact with Jimmy as part of a shared joke.

memories.memory_text: I was rolling around in that haystack, all three of the Taylor Triplets on me at once—laughing, pulling, biting—like a goddamn three-headed love monster.
grounded_facts: Charlie is physically wrestling with the identical Taylor Triplets in a haystack.
The interaction appears playful and physically chaotic.

memories.memory_text: I saw the triplets' kids peeking out from the haystack—many little versions of me—and I wasn't sure what to feel.
grounded_facts: The Taylor Triplets' children are seen peeking out from the haystack.
Charlie observes that the children resemble him.

memories.scene_title: 8、INT. TEACHER'S LOUNGE - TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - DAY
memories.memory_text: I was crouched in the air vent, camera in hand, about to snap another shot—then the whole damn thing gave way and crashed onto the coffee machine. The second I saw all those teachers staring up at me, I knew I was screwed.
grounded_facts: Julie was inside an air vent in the teacher's lounge.
She was holding a digital camera when the vent collapsed.
The collapse caused a loud crash, drawing immediate attention from everyone in the room.

memories.scene_title: 8、INT. TEACHER'S LOUNGE - TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - DAY
memories.memory_text: Merkin didn’t even yell at first—just locked eyes with me after seeing the magazine, and I could tell he finally put it together: I was the one behind 'EXPOSED!'. That quiet second before he spoke felt like a verdict.
grounded_facts: Principal Merkin had just spilled copies of 'EXPOSED!' before Julie was discovered.
He was already angry and searching for the person responsible.
Julie recognized the shift in his expression as he connected her presence to the magazine.

memories.memory_text: I leaned back, feet up on his precious files, and blew a bubble big enough to make my point—this whole setup was a joke, and he knew it.
grounded_facts: Julie sits in front of Ted's desk and leans back with her feet on his files. She blows a large bubble while sarcastically commenting on Ted's journalism. Her posture and actions are deliberately disrespectful and defiant. She mocks Ted's reporting on bake sales and trash pickups. She questions whether ignorance is bliss
delivering the line with a deadpan expression.

memories.memory_text: I told him to go ahead and ask her to the prom—I triple-dog-dared him, just to watch the train wreck unfold.
grounded_facts: Julie notices Ted looking at Christy in the newsroom. She mocks his attraction by referring to Christy as "Miss Titty Pom Poms." She encourages Ted to ask Christy to the prom. She issues a "triple-dog-dare
which Ted later references in voiceover as the reason for his actions. The dare is delivered with clear sarcasm and provocation. ],

memories.scene_title: 10、INT. TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - MEN'S ROOM - DAY
memories.memory_text: I told Ted it was for his own good, even if it was a twisted way to humiliate him—because someone had to show him the truth, and he never would’ve seen it otherwise.
grounded_facts: Julie issued a dare that led Ted to vomit in the sink.
Julie justified the dare as being 'for his own good' in a 'twisted sort of publicly humiliating way'.
Julie spoke sarcastically and detachedly about Ted's reaction.

memories.memory_text: I saw him on TV, the way he carried himself now—smug, posturing—and all I could think was, he reinvented himself into a dick.
grounded_facts: Julie is watching television in her bedroom.
She observes Mr. Nelson's transformed behavior on screen.
She reacts with stunned disbelief and verbalizes her judgment of him.

memories.memory_text: I watched Ted stumble out of that ridiculous cloud-painted helicopter like he was starring in his own movie, then actually say 'What gives, Don Corleone?' to Charlie, who looked like he was trying way too hard in his mobster-lite getup.
grounded_facts: Julie observed Ted exiting a helicopter in slow motion and stumbling slightly.
Julie saw Charlie kiss the pilot publicly and noted his mafioso-inspired appearance.
Julie addressed Charlie with sarcasm, calling him 'Don Corleone' as a critique of his pretentious behavior.
Julie made a sarcastic remark about new shoes not making a new man, responding to Charlie's claim of reinvention.

memories.memory_text: I told Ted his editorial criticism didn’t mean a thing—just because he’s rich now doesn’t make him smarter or better than me.
grounded_facts: Julie responded to Ted's criticism by asserting her superiority and dismissing his newfound status.
She emphasized that his wealth hadn't changed his intelligence or skill.

memories.memory_text: When he tried to push me around, I shot back that I’d write a story about him—'Local boy inherits more money than god'—and watched him actually get excited about it.
grounded_facts: Julie threatened to write a human interest story about Ted's inheritance as a countermove to his authority.
She used sarcasm in framing the story idea, referencing his wealth in a mocking tone.
Ted reacted positively to the idea, expressing interest.

memories.memory_text: I watched Ted on that stupid talk show, all polished and grinning, talking about 'expanding beauty' while pinning some dumb rose to his jacket like he was doing something noble. 'The Girls of Xenia'? Jesus.
grounded_facts: Julie observed Ted during his televised interview on The Tonight Show.
Ted announced a pictorial titled 'The Girls of Xenia' as a tribute to his hometown.
Julie expressed visible disgust and muttered 'The Girls of Xenia? Jesus.'
Ted wore a rose from Ginger on his lapel during the interview.

memories.memory_text: I stood up in the council hall and called out Merkin, asking if he only cared about 'Heaven' because it was too close to home—he actually agreed with me, which was almost disappointing.
grounded_facts: Julie was in the crowd at the city council hall, taking notes.
Julie spoke during the public debate, directly addressing Principal Merkin with a pointed question.
Principal Merkin responded to Julie by confirming her accusation that his concern was personal.

memories.memory_text: I snapped a flash photo of Ted and Ginger mid-dance, catching her with lipstick on her teeth—her face went from dreamy to furious in a second.
grounded_facts: Julie took a flash photograph of Ted and Ginger while they were dancing. She pointed out to Ginger that she had lipstick on her teeth. Ginger reacted with irritation
saying
You could have warned us." ],

memories.memory_text: Ted gave me some dumb compliment about cleaning up nice, so I shot back with William Randolph to shut it down—he knows better than to pretend I’m just another girl here.
grounded_facts: Ted said to Julie

Wow...you clean up nice." Julie responded sarcastically, calling him "William Randolph" and reminding him she was on assignment. Julie was wearing a deep purple strapless gown, noted as natural and appealing. ],

memories.memory_text: I cut through the crowd on the Harley, engine roaring, and stopped right in front of him—dress flapping, jacket zipped up. Said, 'Need a lift?' like it was no big deal, even though my heart was doing backflips.
grounded_facts: Julie arrived on a Harley motorcycle outside the Holiday Inn at night.
She was wearing her purple strapless prom dress with a black motorcycle jacket over it.
She directly addressed Ted with the line 'Need a lift?'
Ted responded by getting on the motorcycle behind her and wrapping his arms around her waist.

memories.memory_text: I took the wheel and drove us out past Xenia, just me and Ted, heading down a dark country road with no destination he could guess. I told him it was the best hiding place in the world, and for once, I meant it without irony.
grounded_facts: Julie is driving at night through Xenia and onto a country road.
Ted asks where they are going, indicating he does not know the destination.
Julie responds with a voice-over line stating they are going to 'the best hiding place in the world.'

memories.memory_text: I remember the oak tree in the backyard—big enough to hide in, just like the rest of this oversized life I never asked for.
grounded_facts: Julie's family is wealthy, as indicated by the opulent backyard setting.
The backyard is large and contains a sprawling oak tree.
The oak tree is described as the best hiding place in the world, suggesting personal significance or familiarity.

memories.memory_text: He didn’t build this place—he had it built, like it was just another transaction. I could hear the hollow pride in Ted’s voice, mistaking money for love.
grounded_facts: Julie corrects Ted when he says her father built the tree house, stating 'He didn't. He had it built.'
Julie emphasizes the difference between personal effort and delegated action, rejecting the conflation of wealth with care.
The tree house is described as cozy with books and flashlights, but Julie associates it with emotional absence.

memories.memory_text: He kept pushing me to expose Merk, like I owe him rebellion. But I told him—lemons into lemonade. I’m not chasing scandals just to prove I’m still sharp.
grounded_facts: Ted pressures Julie to investigate and expose Principal Merk, referencing her restricted journalism assignments.
Julie responds with 'A good journalist can take lemons and make lemonade. I'm honing my craft,' asserting her professional integrity.
Julie explicitly states she is 'out of the "exposed" business' and needs to graduate, showing her restraint and focus.

memories.memory_text: I watched Merkin in that ridiculous mustache, grinning like an idiot while crooning with a bunch of old people, and for a second, I couldn’t tell if we were the ones chasing justice or just a couple of assholes looking for a villain who didn’t exist.
grounded_facts: Julie observed Merkin dressed in a barber shop quartet costume with a handlebar mustache.
Julie saw Merkin singing joyfully while senior citizens clapped.
Julie was with Ted, peeking through a crack in the doorway.
Julie felt disappointment upon seeing Merkin's genuine enjoyment.

memories.memory_text: I watched Merkin serve soup to that homeless guy, then gave Ted a look—like, 'See? This is what I meant.'
grounded_facts: Julie observes Principal Merkin pouring soup for a shabbily dressed homeless man.
Julie directs an 'I told you so' look toward Ted.

memories.memory_text: I called Ted and told him I had an anonymous tip with an address in Cincinnati—felt like crossing a line, but I said 'Let's do it' before he could ask questions.
grounded_facts: Julie initiated a phone call with Ted, asking 'Where the hell are you?'
Julie reported receiving an anonymous tip and provided an address in Cincinnati.
Julie used the phrase 'Let's do it' to confirm action.

memories.memory_text: I felt the heat of Ted leaning in, his voice dropping low like he was trying to hypnotize me with words, and for a second I almost believed him—until my eyes caught Merkin in full Barbra drag under the spotlight.
grounded_facts: Ted leaned closer and spoke in a seductive
aurally focused way while asking Julie to trust him. Julie responded with hesitation
saying 'Maybe someday
and did not pull away physically. At the moment their intimacy peaked, Julie noticed Principal Merkin on stage in drag, causing her to grab Ted
s hand and react in shock. The transition from personal tension to public revelation was abrupt and visually jarring due to the stage lighting and Merkin's appearance.

memories.memory_text: I was laughing in the rain with Ted, soaked and breathless, and for a second I let myself feel that stupid, giddy high—like any of it meant something.
grounded_facts: Julie was rushing down the street with Ted in the rain, giggling and euphoric.
They stopped, caught their breath, and began kissing while drenched.

memories.memory_text: I pulled away when he brought up that pictorial again—I had to say it straight: I hate 'The Girls of Xenia,' and the way he talks about it like it's just another job makes me wonder if he sees any of this as real.
grounded_facts: Julie sobers and pulls away from Ted after he mentions the 'Girls of Xenia' centerfold.
She explicitly states her disdain for the pictorial, not just the town's mentality.
She questions Ted's arousal and motives, expressing deep discomfort.

memories.memory_text: I ran from Ted, water dripping down his face, calling after me—begging me to let him explain, like that would fix anything.
grounded_facts: Julie exits the girls' locker room and passes a janitor.
Ted is wet and follows her, calling out 'At least let me explain!'.
Julie enters the girls' restroom to escape Ted.
Ted pauses, then follows her into the restroom after realizing no one else is around.

memories.memory_text: I unzipped my dress and let it drop, standing there naked in front of him—not because I wanted him to want me, but because I needed him to see how little it meant, how empty he really was.
grounded_facts: Julie unzips the back of her summery dress and lets it fall to the floor.
She stands completely naked in front of Ted near a row of sinks.
She speaks about nudity having nothing to do with emotion or love.

memories.memory_text: I told him he didn’t know me at all, not even after seeing me naked—his magazine, his crush, all of it just noise, and I liked him better before he became this hollow version of himself.
grounded_facts: Julie states that Ted doesn’t know her even one ounce better after seeing her naked.
She criticizes Ted’s magazine and says she liked him better when he was sweet, kind, and human.
She speaks directly to Ted before walking away.

memories.memory_text: Charlie stood there grinning, handing me a copy of 'Heaven'—some magazine he clearly thought mattered. I flipped to page three and saw it: 'To Julie, My Partner in Crime, With All the Love in the World.' My eyes stung before I could stop them.
grounded_facts: Julie opened her front door and encountered Charlie.
Charlie handed her a copy of 'Heaven'.
Julie turned to page three and read a personal dedication from Charlie addressed to her.
The dedication used the phrase 'Partner in Crime' and 'With All the Love in the World'.
Julie's eyes filled with tears upon reading it.

memories.memory_text: I flipped ahead to check the 'Girls of Xenia' spread and froze—there was Principal Merkin in full drag, belting out a Barbra Streisand number. And in some shots, Molly was right there with him, like they were posing together. I muttered something about yellow journalism just to keep my voice steady.
grounded_facts: Julie turned to the 'Girls of Xenia' pictorial in the magazine.
She saw photographs of Principal Merkin performing in drag as Barbra Streisand.
The photos were from a Cincinnati nightclub.
Other images included Molly in the foreground, positioned suggestively with Merkin.
Julie commented sarcastically that Charlie 'has a future in yellow journalism'.

memories.memory_text: I heard Ted say he was giving up the magazine to go to college, and when he joked about being a coxswain, I couldn’t help but say, ‘Very ambitious. I like that.’ It was the first time I believed he wasn’t just playing a role.
grounded_facts: Julie hears Ted say he decided to let Helen run the magazine and that he wants to go to college.
Ted mentions wanting to be a 'coxswain,' referencing earlier dialogue from Jimmy and Charlie.
Julie responds with 'Very ambitious. I like that,' indicating approval.

memories.memory_text: The song 'People' came on, and Ted held out his hand, saying it was our song. I took it and said, 'You better,' not knowing if I meant the dance or something more—but for once, I didn’t pull away.
grounded_facts: The music changes to 'People,' which Ted calls 'our song.'
Ted extends his hand and asks Julie to dance.
Julie takes his hand and responds with 'You better,' before they begin dancing and kissing.

memories.memory_text: I stood on the deck of the sailboat, the wind sharp against my face, watching the sun bleed gold into the Pacific while Ted steered us into the glare—like some punchline to a romance we never actually wrote.
grounded_facts: Julie is on a sailboat with Ted off the Pacific Coast.
The scene takes place during a sunset.
Julie is present and observant during the sailboat journey.

memories.memory_text: I was in the middle of a dramatic exit when this guy knocked into me, and then he just blurts out that my biological parents died in the Amazon from piranhas—like it was nothing.
grounded_facts: Ted was interrupted during a melodramatic exit.
Marty Zukerman delivered news about Ted's biological parents dying in the Amazon.
The cause of death mentioned was piranhas while skinny-dipping.
Ted initially thought the man was referring to his adoptive parents and expressed disbelief.

memories.memory_text: I remember saying, 'They didn't want me when I was born. Why should I care... if they're dead,' and feeling the weight of it hang in the air, candles flickering like the silence after.
grounded_facts: Ted says his grandparents did not want him at birth.
Ted questions why he should care about his family legacy or their death.
The room is lit by candles with rain outside during this conversation.
Ted's father is present on the edge of the bed, responding to Ted's statement.

memories.memory_text: I snapped at Charlie, 'Oh, how inappropriate is that!' when he started pawing through my mother's lingerie like it was some sick trophy case.
grounded_facts: Ted expresses disgust when Charlie opens a drawer filled with lingerie belonging to Ted's biological mother.
Ted verbally confronts Charlie with 'Oh, how inappropriate is that!' in response to Charlie handling the panties.

memories.memory_text: After we ripped the panties apart fighting over them, the butler walked in and we both stood there like guilty idiots, hiding the pieces behind our backs.
grounded_facts: Ted grabs the panties from Charlie, causing them to tear in half.
The English butler enters unexpectedly, sees the two acting suspiciously, and exits with a polite assumption.
Ted and Charlie hide the torn halves of the panties behind their backs in a moment of awkward silence.

memories.memory_text: I saw the price tag on the sleeve and couldn't believe the number—my whole life I'd never seen that many zeros on a receipt.
grounded_facts: Ted sees the price tag dangling from his sleeve after emerging from the dressing room.
His eyes widen, indicating shock or disbelief at the price.

memories.memory_text: Ginger whispered in my ear about money only mattering to those who don't have it—I felt her breath, the smoothness of her voice, like it was supposed to mean something bigger than just clothes.
grounded_facts: Ginger whispers seductively in Ted's ear after he emerges from the dressing room.
She says: 'Your father always said, "Money matters only to the man who doesn't have it."'
Ted listens and is trying to learn, indicating he is absorbing the moment.

memories.memory_text: I stood up in front of all those reporters, lights blazing, and said I’d been editor of the high school paper in Xenia, Ohio—won awards, made a difference. Felt proud saying it out loud, like I finally had permission to matter.
grounded_facts: Ted identifies himself as the editor of the high school paper in Xenia, Ohio.
He states he won awards and made a difference during his time on the paper.
He expresses pride while speaking and connects journalism to his identity.

memories.memory_text: I joked about having my dad’s taste in women and cars—faster, the better—and winked. The room chuckled, Billy gave me a thumbs-up, and for a second, it felt like we were in on the same joke.
grounded_facts: Ted makes a comment about inheriting his father's taste in women and cars.
He winks while delivering the line.
The crowd reacts with chuckles, and Uncle Billy responds with a thumbs-up.

memories.memory_text: I told Merkin that turning down the magazine would be insane—this was my dream, the kind every guy would kill for.
grounded_facts: Ted defends his decision to accept the magazine offer by calling it his dream and appealing to a shared male fantasy. He responds defiantly to Merkin's disapproval with sarcasm and rhetorical questions. The dialogue occurs in Merkin's office after being praised for his editorial work.

memories.memory_text: I reminded him about the Rolex I sent, and watching him reluctantly hand it back—I could tell it killed him to do it.
grounded_facts: Ted questions whether Merkin still wants the Rolex he previously sent. Merkin opens his desk drawer
caresses the watch
and returns it with visible reluctance. The moment underscores Merkin's hypocrisy after claiming money means nothing.

memories.memory_text: I remember hearing Uncle Billy call me the 'Titan of Tushies' and laughing—really laughing—because for once, someone important was praising me like I mattered, like I was built for something big.
grounded_facts: Uncle Billy praises Ted by calling him
the Titan of Tushies" and "General of Jism
Ted laughs in response to the praise
The praise comes during a video conference in Ted's modified bedroom

memories.memory_text: My dad stood in the doorway with that chore list again, and I just nodded and turned back to the screen—couldn't let him interrupt the moment, even if I saw how he hesitated before leaving.
grounded_facts: Ted's dad appears at the door holding a chore list
Ted takes the list
gives it a cursory glance
and nods
Ted immediately resumes talking to Uncle Billy and Helen
Dad backs out of the room unnoticed by Ted

memories.memory_text: I stood on the ottoman while the tailor fitted me, looking down at them as they begged me to give up the magazine—like I was supposed to trade everything I built just to fit their little dream.
grounded_facts: Ted is being fitted for a prom tuxedo while standing on an ottoman, physically towering over his parents.
His parents express disapproval of the magazine and ask him to give it up, wanting him to go to college and live a conventional life.
Ted responds defiantly, rejecting their dream and asserting his own vision.

memories.memory_text: I told them I was never meant to be raised by grocers—and when I stormed off, I knocked over the family photo, the glass shattering like something final, though I didn’t mean to break it.
grounded_facts: Ted lashes out by referencing his adoption and calling his parents grocers, emphasizing his feeling of being out of place.
He storms away and accidentally crashes into a treasured family photo, causing the frame to shatter.
The moment is marked by stunned silence, indicating emotional rupture.

memories.memory_text: I had all four penthouse suites booked under my name, Dom Perignon in hand, ordering a hundred and seventeen of those ugly gnomes and ten dozen springy-head puppies—this was my show, and I wasn’t just crashing the prom, I was rewriting it.
grounded_facts: Ted rented all four penthouse suites.
Ted was drinking Dom Perignon.
Ted placed a large order for ceramic gnomes and springy-head puppies via phone during the party.
The party was chaotic, with kids dancing, playing drinking games, and trashing the suites.

memories.memory_text: Merkin showed up like some buzzkill prophet, talking about laws and developing minds—like we weren’t all just kids trying to feel alive for one night—and when he said he called the cops, I swear the whole floor dropped, but I made sure he saw the look before I ran.
grounded_facts: Principal Merkin arrived at the party.
Merkin stated that Ted broke school board rules and the law.
Merkin mentioned the state's zero tolerance policy for underage drinking.
Merkin confirmed he called the police.
The Xenia Police Force arrived in riot gear, ending the party.
Ted gave Merkin a spiteful look before fleeing.

memories.memory_text: I was just walking out with the crowd after prom, still buzzing from the night, when Julie came out of nowhere on that Harley—purple dress, leather jacket, completely unexpected. She looked back and said, 'Need a lift?'—and before I could overthink it, I was on the bike, holding onto her, wind rushing as we sped off.
grounded_facts: Ted was exiting the Holiday Inn with other prom attendees.
Julie arrived on a Harley motorcycle wearing her prom dress and a black motorcycle jacket.
Julie asked Ted, 'Need a lift?'
Ted got on the motorcycle behind Julie and held onto her waist.
They drove away together immediately after.

memories.memory_text: I asked Julie if my real father would’ve just bought me a stadium instead of teaching me how to play ball—like her dad phoned in the treehouse, maybe mine would’ve done the same.
grounded_facts: Ted questions whether his biological father would have been emotionally absent, using Julie’s treehouse as a metaphor.
He contrasts a father who provides materially but not emotionally with a dad who teaches and connects.

memories.memory_text: I told Julie that even when she infuriates me, I still want to kiss her. She said I never do.
grounded_facts: Ted expresses that he still wants to kiss Julie despite her infuriating him.
Julie responds by pointing out that Ted never acts on his feelings.

memories.memory_text: I hung up the phone after Julie told me the target was on the move—Cincinnati, some anonymous tip—and all I said was, 'Let's do it.'
grounded_facts: Ted receives a call from Julie in Julie's bedroom at night.
Julie informs Ted that the target is on the move and provides an address in Cincinnati from an anonymous tip.
Ted responds with 'Let's do it' and hangs up the phone.

memories.memory_text: I was soaked and laughing with Julie in the rain after finding the perfect centerfold, but then she turned serious and said she despised 'The Girls of Xenia'—my own project—and I had to tell her it was about keeping a promise, not arousal.
grounded_facts: Ted and Julie were running in the rain, laughing and drenched.
Julie expressed that she profoundly disliked Ted's pictorial, 'The Girls of Xenia'.
Ted defended his work by comparing it to a professional duty and emphasized his commitment to keeping his word.
Julie acknowledged that she could respect him for that.

memories.scene_title: 84、INT. TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - GIRL'S LOCKER ROOM - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I remember unzipping Christy’s top, my hands slow on the zipper, catching that first glimpse of her bare back—smooth, long, and glowing under the strobe lights. She didn’t say a word, just gave me that coy look over her shoulder, like she’d been waiting for this exact moment.
grounded_facts: Ted unzips Christy's cheerleader top in the shower area of the girl's locker room.
Christy is posing in her underwear, wearing only panties after removing her top and skirt.
The scene is lit with reflectors and strobes, creating a staged, sensual atmosphere.
Ted observes Christy's body and reacts silently, overwhelmed.

memories.scene_title: 84、INT. TECUMSEH HIGH SCHOOL - GIRL'S LOCKER ROOM - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I remember her from second grade—blue gingham dress, pulling her ponytail. Now she's here in the shower, unzipping her top, taking off her clothes. I'm outside the shower, squirming in my clothes, camera in hand, not sure what comes next.
grounded_facts: Ted recalls Christy from second grade, specifically her appearance and an incident where he pulled her hair.
Christy unzips her top and removes her cheerleader uniform, now wearing only small panties.
Ted is outside the shower, no longer in Christy's sight.
Ted is physically uncomfortable or fidgeting ('squirming in his clothes'), indicating internal tension.
Ted is holding his camera and observing Christy posing for photos.

memories.memory_text: I was still dripping from the shower when I chased Julie down the hall, yelling for her to wait—just let me explain—but she bolted into the girls’ restroom like I was the last person she ever wanted to see. I stood there, alone in the quiet, then stepped inside after her.
grounded_facts: Ted was wet from being in the shower and followed Julie through the school corridor.
Ted called out to Julie, asking her to let him explain something.
Julie entered the girls' restroom and Ted entered it afterward, noting he was alone.

memories.memory_text: I saw Julie undress right in front of me, standing there naked by the sinks, and I couldn’t look away—not because of her body, but because I actually cared about her.
grounded_facts: Julie undressed in front of Ted in the girls' restroom.
Ted gasped and was visibly affected, despite having seen naked women before.
The moment was emotionally significant to Ted because of his feelings for Julie.

memories.memory_text: Her dress was back on before I could say anything, and then my phone rang—Uncle Billy screaming about the magazine, while she just walked away, like I’d already become one of the fake things she hated.
grounded_facts: Julie put her dress back on and dismissed Ted’s connection to her.
Ted’s phone rang as Julie left, with Uncle Billy reporting that readers hated the magazine.
Julie rejected Ted, associating him with the inauthenticity of the magazine project.

memories.memory_text: I sat there in the conference room and heard Uncle Billy call me 'the kid' like I wasn't even a real part of the team, like none of my ideas had ever mattered.
grounded_facts: Uncle Billy refers to Ted as 'the kid' during the meeting.
Ted visibly reacts with offense ('Ted bristles') upon being called 'the kid'.
The comment is made in front of department heads, diminishing Ted's professional standing.

memories.memory_text: I realized right then that Billy was letting me take the fall for the magazine issue, shifting all the blame onto me even though I thought we were on the same page.
grounded_facts: Uncle Billy attributes the failed idea to Ted ('The kid came up with something that sounded good on paper').
Ted observes that Billy is presenting the situation differently than expected ('Ted can see that Uncle Billy's playing this out differently than he imagined').
Ted understands he is being scapegoated as the sole responsible party for the magazine's failure.

memories.memory_text: I was sitting at my father's desk, buried in reports and charts, when the sound of girls laughing by the pool pulled me to the window—watching them, I felt hollow, not happy, and I realized the life I built wasn't what I thought it would be.
grounded_facts: Ted was sitting at his father's desk going through paperwork.
Ted heard playful sounds of women and approached the window to watch girls splashing in the pool.
Ted expressed sadness and disillusionment, stating that his dreams of fame, fortune, and beautiful women had brought him grief, not fulfillment.

memories.memory_text: I remember seeing those baby photos in the scrapbook—me through a nursery window, like they were watching from a distance, never stepping in. I never knew they’d been keeping track all along.
grounded_facts: Helen shows Ted a scrapbook containing photos of him as a baby taken through a nursery window
The scrapbook also includes a newspaper photo of Ted in a nativity play and clippings from his high school newspaper
Ted expresses surprise that his biological parents knew who he was and had followed his life

memories.memory_text: I stood there holding the weight of that empire they built for me, and all I could think was: my real parents are in Xenia, I’ve hurt them, and the girl I love would never look at me the same if I took this.
grounded_facts: Helen reveals that Ted’s biological parents founded 'Heaven' and intended it as his inheritance
Ted expresses conflict over loyalty to his adoptive parents in Xenia and emotional pain over having hurt them
Ted mentions that the girl he loves hates 'Heaven
and he fears losing her respect if he accepts the legacy

memories.memory_text: I stood up from the couch, my eyes wet, and told them everything I am is because of you—then asked if they could ever forgive me, staring at the restored portrait above the mantle.
grounded_facts: Ted rises from the couch when his parents and Jimmy enter.
Ted speaks emotionally, saying 'Everything I am is because of you. Can you ever forgive me?'
The large restored family portrait is hanging over the mantle, which he previously broke.

memories.memory_text: After Mom scolded me for saying 'shit,' I told them I'm really in deep shit and need their help—knowing I couldn't fix this alone, despite how much I hate admitting it.
grounded_facts: Ted says, 'I need your help. I'm really in deep shit.'
Mom corrects his language, saying 'Don’t say “shit,” honey.'
Dad responds by assuring there's no problem too deep or too shitty they can't solve together.

memories.memory_text: I burst into the stockholders' meeting holding up a can of peaches, my dad right behind me, and shouted over Uncle Billy—suddenly every eye was on me, confused, waiting to see if I’d choke. I could feel my hands shaking, but I made myself walk to the podium and say what needed to be said about canned peaches and keeping promises.
grounded_facts: Ted enters the meeting holding a can of peaches and interrupts Uncle Billy's speech.
Ted addresses the stockholders, referencing his adoptive father and grandfather's connection to peaches.
Ted admits he made a mistake by replacing canned peaches with other fruits.
The stockholders respond with a standing ovation after his speech.

memories.memory_text: After the applause, I turned to Uncle Billy and told him family is about love, not blood—then fired him, watching the grin drop off his face as he begged for mercy. I didn’t flinch, even when he called me 'Ted-baby' and reminded me he was my uncle.
grounded_facts: Ted confronts Uncle Billy after receiving the standing ovation.
Ted states that family is defined by love, not blood.
Ted fires Uncle Billy, who pleads for leniency.
Uncle Billy appeals to their familial relationship.

memories.memory_text: I watched the final layouts, listened to the team, and felt this quiet certainty—like I wasn’t just playing a role anymore. When they said the magazine was going to press, it didn’t feel like luck. It felt like I’d earned it.
grounded_facts: Ted observes pictures, layouts, and copy for the magazine.
Ted listens to his staff and appears confident and mature.
The magazine goes to press, marking a culmination of his work.

memories.memory_text: Someone handed me Julie’s article, and I read the last line out loud—'remember, always, where he came from.' I don’t know if I believed I deserved those words, but for once, I didn’t make a joke. I just stood there, quiet.
grounded_facts: Julie's article about Ted is read in voiceover while he is present.
Ted is handed a copy of the article and reads part of it aloud.
The article reflects on Ted's journey and character with empathy and respect.

memories.memory_text: I told Julie I was giving up the magazine, going to college, and wanted her as my first mate—she smiled, and for the first time, I meant it when I said I wanted to be a coxswain.
grounded_facts: Ted says he decided to let Helen run the magazine and that he wants to go to college.
Ted uses the metaphor of needing a first mate, directly addressing Julie.
Julie responds with a pleased expression and a teasing remark about naked girls.

memories.memory_text: I asked Julie to dance when 'People' came on—she took my hand, we kissed, and I felt like everything I ever wanted wasn't money or power, but just this.
grounded_facts: The song 'People' starts playing at the party.
Ted extends his hand and says, 'Dare I ask for this dance?'
Julie takes his hand, they begin dancing and kissing in the background.

memories.memory_text: I remember the sun bleeding gold and red into the Pacific as we cut through the waves—Julie at the helm, me beside her, not saying much, just letting the silence say it all.
grounded_facts: Ted is on a sailboat with Julie.
The scene takes place during the day, ending in a glorious sunset.
Ted and Julie are sailing off together into the sunset.
The moment is quiet, with no dialogue indicated in the scene text.
